---
title: "Lesson 2 RStudio"
author: "Paul Lott"
date: "2024-05-13"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# 1. RStudio IDE

![](https://images.datacamp.com/image/upload/v1663778862/R_Studio_Interface_1336eacbe7.png)RStudio
is not just a wrapper for the standard R console - it is an full fledged
integrated development environment (IDE) for the R programming language.
Some of its features include:

Customizable workbench with all of the tools required to work with R in
one place (console, source, plots, workspace, help, history, etc.).

-   Syntax highlighting editor with code completion.

-   Execute code directly from the source editor (line, selection, or
    file).

-   Code Debugging

-   Full support for authoring Sweave and TeX documents.

-   Runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux, and has a community-maintained
    FreeBSD port.

-   Can also be run as a server, enabling multiple users to access the
    RStudio IDE using a web browser

Let's spend some time getting familiar with RStudio

-   [RStudio
    Cheatsheet](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/html/rstudio-ide.html)

Goto *File\>Help\>Cheatsheets\>RStudio IDE Cheatsheet* to open the main
Rstudio Cheatsheet

### Getting Familiar with the Windows of RStudio

-   **Upper Left**
    -   Script/Code Editor Window
-   **Lower Left**
    -   Console - R Console (Same as in RGui/Command Line) This is your
        R session.
    -   Terminal - Bash/OS Terminal
    -   Background Jobs -
    -   Render -
-   **Upper Right**
    -   Environment
    -   History
    -   Connections
    -   Tutorial
-   **Lower Right**
    -   Files
    -   Plots
    -   Packages
    -   Help
    -   Viewer

Additional Support:

-   <https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/>

-   <https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/sections/200107586-Using-the-RStudio-IDE>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Getting Started in RStudio IDE

## Projects

Once you've got Rstudio opened, you can create a new project or open an
existing project.

**Projects** in Rstudio provide an easy way to split your different
analysis into separate environments and sessions. **Projects** can be
created in a new directory, existing directory, or *checkout* a project
from a code repository such as Github.

### [Rstudio Projects](https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/articles/200526207-Using-RStudio-Projects)

When a project is opened within RStudio the following actions are taken:

-   A new R session (process) is started

-   The .Rprofile file in the project's main directory (if any) is
    sourced by R

-   The .RData file in the project's main directory is loaded (if
    project options indicate that it should be loaded).

-   The .Rhistory file in the project's main directory is loaded into
    the RStudio History pane (and used for Console Up/Down arrow command
    history).

-   The current working directory is set to the project directory.

-   Previously edited source documents are restored into editor tabs

-   Other RStudio settings (e.g. active tabs, splitter positions, etc.)
    are restored to where they were the last time the project was
    closed.

### Quitting a Project

When you are within a project and choose to either Quit, close the
project, or open another project the following actions are taken:

-   .RData and/or .Rhistory are written to the project directory (if
    current options indicate they should be)

-   The list of open source documents is saved (so it can be restored
    next time the project is opened)

-   Other RStudio settings (as described above) are saved.

-   The R session is terminated.

To create a new **Project** directory: *File-\>New Project* (If you
already have something open, you'll be prompted to save the current
session history/results before creating a new session)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. To R Script or R Markdown

When creating a new R file *File-\>New File* you'll notice that you have
the option to create an R script, R Notebook, or R Markdown.

You can think of these as similar to:

**R script** - is equivalent to a Python .py file or other source code
files. It contains the basic script/program but doesn't provide any
place to format your results.

**Example:**

``` r
# store the current directory
initial.dir<-getwd()
# change to the new directory
setwd("/home/jim/psych/risk/adol")
# load the necessary libraries
library(nlme)
# set the output file
sink("adolrisk03.out")
```

**R Notebook/Markdown** - allows you to interactively execute code
blocks and intersperse the code blocks with formatted text, images,
tables, or other Markdown. The page can then be rendered to HTML, PDF or
Word document, so you can not only share your code but also the results.

The difference between a R Markdown document vs R Notebook is that when
running a R Markdown document all the code is sent to the console all at
once. Whereas, the R Notebook only executes one line at a time.

Unless you are developing a package, running code in a non-interactive
setting - it's recommended that you get use to using **R Notebooks.** If
you're creating a report, you'd use a **R Markdown** **Document**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## R Markdown (The Syntax)

R Markdown syntax provides an authoring framework for Researchers. You
can use a single RMarkdown file to write, execute, and save code - as
well, as generate high quality reports that can be shared with others.

-   [R Markdown Tutorial](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/lesson-1.html)
-   [R Markdown
    Cheatsheet](https://www.rstudio.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/rmarkdown-reference.pdf)
-   [Book] [R Markdown: A Definitive Guide
    Guide](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/)
-   [Book] [R Markdown
    Cookbook](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown-cookbook/)

Goto *File\>Help\>Cheatsheets\>RMarkdown Cheatsheet* to open the main
RMarkdown Cheatsheet

These lessons are written completely in R Markdown. You'll notice that
anytime we want to run some code, we need to create a code block use:

````         

```{r}
#Your Code 

``` 
````

### Let's Get Comfortable with R Markdown/Markdown syntax

I mentioned Markdown and you've seen the Notebooks that used Markdown to
format text cells.

However, Markdown is such an important tool for Bioinformatics and
Genomics, we're going to take some time and work through the RMarkdown
Tutorial. Learning Markdown, ensure that you be able to produce
reproducible results whether you're working in Python or R.

RStudio's creator has a good Tutorial on R Markdown - Let's follow the
links and learn more about why we should be using R Markdown for our
projects.

```{r}
#Knit PDF
install.packages("tinytex")
tinytex::install_tinytex()
```

1.  [Introduction](RMarkdown_Lesson-1.Rmd)
2.  [How It Works](RMarkdown_Lesson-2.Rmd)
    -   [Example 1](RMarkdown_Example1.Rmd)
3.  [Code Chunks](RMarkdown_Lesson-3.Rmd)
    -   [Example 2](RMarkdown_Example2.Rmd)
4.  [Inline Code](RMarkdown_Lesson-4.Rmd)
    -   [Example 3](RMarkdown_Example3.Rmd)
5.  [Code Languages](RMarkdown_Lesson-5.Rmd)
    -   [Example 4](RMarkdown_Example4.Rmd)
6.  [Parameters](RMarkdown_Lesson-6.Rmd)
    -   [Example 5](RMarkdown_Example5.Rmd)
7.  [Tables](RMarkdown_Lesson-7.Rmd)
    -   [Example 6](RMarkdown_Example6.Rmd)
8.  [Markdown Basics](RMarkdown_Lesson-8.Rmd)
    -   [Example 7](RMarkdown_Example7.Rmd)
9.  [Output Formats](RMarkdown_Lesson-9.Rmd)
    -   [Example 8](RMarkdown_Example8.Rmd)
10. [Notebooks](RMarkdown_Lesson-10.Rmd)
    -   [Example 9](RMarkdown_Example9.Rmd)
11. [Extra - Slide Presentations](RMarkdown_Lesson-11.Rmd)
12. [Extra - Dashboards](RMarkdown_Lesson-12.Rmd)
13. [Extra - Websites](RMarkdown_Lesson-13.Rmd)
14. [Extra - Interactive Documents](RMarkdown_Lesson-14.Rmd)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Installing and Loading R Packages

The core libraries (ie Standard Libraries) provides basic functionality.
But research community have developed a large huge number of packages
that makes R a fundamental tool in research. R calles these *Packages*,
whereas we called them *Modules* in Python

You can find many packages hosted by the [Comprehensive R Archive
Network (CRAN)](https://cran.r-project.org/), which is the default R
repository.

From the Menu, you can install a package by going to *Tools-\>Install
Packages* or via R console

To install packages from CRAN, you use
`install.packages("<packages name>")`:

```{r eval=FALSE}
install.packages("RSQLite")
```

However, for Genomics the [Bioconductor](https://www.bioconductor.org/)
project has become a fundamental repository for Genomics.

```{r eval=FALSE}
install.packages("BiocManager", dependencies=TRUE)
```

Once Bioconductor Manager is installed, you can install a Bioconductor
package such as `GenomicRanges`

```{r eval=FALSE}
BiocManager::install("GenomicRanges")
```

You can also find packages on: -
[Github](https://github.com/trending/r) -
[RForge](https://r-forge.r-project.org/)

Once you've successfully installed a R packages, you'll need to load the
package. In Python, we'd use `import <module_name>`.

To load packages into R, you use `library("<package name>")`

```{r}
library("RSQLite")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Getting Help in R/RStudio

R and Rstudio have built in help functionality similar to Jupyter
Notebooks. There are multiple ways to get help for a function, object,
or topic.

`help(<command>)` - will open the help for the given command or topic in
the Help tab

```{r, eval=FALSE}
help(solve)
```

Alternatively, you can shorten the command to: `?<command>`

`help.start()` - will take you to the beginning help page - where you
can then look through guides or search for a given topic

```{r, eval=FALSE}
help.start()
```

Don't forget about he ***Help*** tab in the (**Lower Right**). It' gives
you access to all your help topics in a easy to navigate interface. It
also provides quick access to **R Manuals.**

If you want further help and infomration, check out
<https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/available_packages_by_name.html>

### Searching for Help Topic

`help.search(<topic/command>)` - will search the help documentation for
the command or topic you're searching for

Alternatively, `??<topic/command>`

```{r, eval=FALSE}
help.search("data.frame")
```

### Getting Examples

Many of the help topics have examples that are provided in the
documentation. Sometimes, just seeing the examples can help you
understand how a function works. If you want to run the examples from a
topic:

`example(<topic>)`

```{r}
example(vector)
```

If you are looking for help or examples on a given packages go to the
Package Webpage and look for Vignettes or Examples

stringr Cran Page:
<https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/stringr/index.html>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# R Files and Directories

### R Current Working Directory

One thing we didn't cover much in the Python section is that programs
typically run in a given directory. The directory that you start a
program in is called the **Current Working Directory**, in order for the
program to find the necessary files it needs to know where to look in
the directory structure.

If you start R in a certain folder, but want to open a file in a
different folder. You'll need to either:

1.  Set the Current Working Directory to where your files is at

2.  Supply a file path to the file (relative or absolute)

Here is an example of the Directory/File structure on different
Operating Systems:

![](https://i.stack.imgur.com/wQUDB.jpg)

**Important:** Linux and Mac OSX have a directory structure that defines
`/` as root. Windows uses **C:** as the boot drive and letters to define
other drives (**B:**, **E:**, **Z:**, ...) . The root directory on the
**C:** drive is then `C:\`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are couple ways to tell a program where the file is:

### Absolute Path

Absolute paths define the complete path and filename of the file. So to
define the path relative to the root directory, we start with '/' or
'C:'.

**For Linux:** `/home/plott/Downloads/MyData.txt`

**For Mac OSX:** `/Users/plott/Downloads/MyData.txt`

**For Windows:** `C:\Users\plott\Downloads\MyData.txt`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Relative Path

Relative paths are defined from where your current working directory
is - which folder the program started in or is set to by default.

When you first started R, it probably started in your Home Directory,
and considers this your Current Working Directory. In the above
examples, I used `/home/plott`, `/Users/plott` or `C:\Users\plott` as my
Home directory for different operating systems.

If I wanted to open `MyScript.txt`, I'd could supply the relative path,
which is relative to my Current Working Directory

**For Linux:** `Downloads/MyData.txt`

**For Mac OSX:** `Downloads/MyData.txt`

**For Windows:**`Downloads/MyData.txt`

Note: Relative to the Current Working Directory - they are all the same
irregardless of Operating System.

What if you wanted to open a file in `/home`, `/Users`, or `C:\Users`
directory, but wanted to use relative path.

The `..` double-period denotes the directory just previous to the
Current Working Directory.

So if there was a file called `SharedData.txt` in the `/home` or
`/Users` or `C:\Users` directory. We'd be able to get to it by:
`../SharedData.txt` - This means first go to the directory just prior to
your Current working directory and look for a file called
`SharedData.txt`

**Note:** If you wanted to go 2 directories up you could utilize `..`
like: `../../SharedDate.txt`

A single period `.` denotes your current directory, so
`./SharedData.txt` is the same as just `SharedData.txt`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### OK. Why do we need to know this

Well, R uses this idea of Current Working Directory. If you want to work
on a file, but R complains that it can't find it. It's often because
your current working directory isn't what you thought.

#### To see your current working directory:

Goto **File** Tab (Lower Right) *More-\>Go To Working Directory*

or

```{r}
getwd()
```

#### To set your current working directory:

Goto **File** Tab (Lower Right) Navigate to the directory you want as
your Working Directory and press *More-\>Set As Working Directory*

or

```{r}
setwd('/home/plott')
```

# File Handling In R

### Listing Files

If you want to identify the files in a particular directory, you can
navigate using the *Files* tab (*Lower-Right Corner*) or

```{r}
list.files(path='.')  #Get the files in your Current directory
```

### Additional R File and Directory Functions

+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| R Function           | Unix Command         | Description          |
+======================+======================+======================+
| getwd()              | pwd                  | Get the current      |
|                      |                      | working directory    |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| s e t w              | cd \<directory\>     |                      |
| d(\<directory\>)     |                      |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| l i s t              | ls                   | Lists all files in a |
| .files(path=".")     |                      | given directory      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| l i s                | ls                   | List directories     |
| t.dirs(path=".")     |                      |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l                | touch \<fn\>         | Create an empty file |
| e.create(\<fn\>)     |                      |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| file.exists(...)     |                      | Returns `TRUE` if    |
|                      |                      | file exists          |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l                | rm \<fn\>            | Delete a file        |
| e.remove(\<fn\>)     |                      |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l e .            | mv \<from\> \<to\>   | Attempts to rename   |
| rename(\<from\>,     |                      | filename `<from>` to |
| \<new\>)             |                      | `<new>`              |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i le.append(file1, | cat \<file1\>        | Appends File1 and    |
| file2)               | \<file2\>            | File2                |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l                | ls -alh              | Provide information  |
| e.info(filename)     |                      | about file (size,    |
|                      |                      | isdir, mode, time,   |
|                      |                      | uid, gui, username,  |
|                      |                      | groupname)           |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l                | cp \<from\> \<to\>   | Copy a file `from`   |
| e.copy(\<from\>,     |                      | file to `new`        |
| \<to\>)              |                      | location             |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| f i l e . s          | ln -sf \<from\>      | Create a symbolic    |
| ymlink(\<from\>,     | \<to\>               | link to a `from`     |
| \<new\>)             |                      | file to `new`        |
|                      |                      | location             |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| dir.exists           |                      | Return `TRUE` if     |
| (\<directory\>)      |                      | directory exists     |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| dir. c r             | mkdir \<newdir\>     | Create a Directory   |
| eate(\<newdir\>)     |                      |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| s y s                | chmod \<permission\> | Change File          |
| .chmod(\<path\>,     | \<path/fn\>          | permissions          |
| mode=\               |                      |                      |
| \< Permission#\>)    |                      | 4 = Read             |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      |                      | 2 = Write            |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      |                      | 1 = Execute          |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      |                      | 7 = (4+2+1) = Read,  |
|                      |                      | Write, Execute       |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      |                      | \<Users#, Group#,    |
|                      |                      | Everyone#\>          |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      |                      | 777 = RWE for User / |
|                      |                      | Group/Everyone       |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| basename(path)       | basename \<path\>    | Returns the Filename |
|                      |                      | portion of the path  |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| dirname(path)        | dirname              | Returns the          |
|                      |                      | Directory portion of |
|                      |                      | the path             |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| unlink(x)            | rm \<directory\>     | Deletes files or     |
|                      |                      | directories          |
|                      | or                   | specified by x       |
|                      |                      |                      |
|                      | rmdir \<directory\>  |                      |
+----------------------+----------------------+----------------------+

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Calling Unix/Linux Apps from Inside R/RStudio

Within R/Rstudio you can run Unix/Linux commands and applications.
Within RStudio R Notebooks/Markdown documents, you'll create a code
block with {bash} instead of {r}. The commands in the code block will be
executed in the Unix Terminal.

```{bash}
cat Lesson1_Intro_to_R.Rmd | sort | uniq | wc
```

Alternatively, if you want to call Unix directly from your R code,
you'll use a `system()` or `system2()` function calls.

```{r}
system('cat Lesson1_Intro_to_R.Rmd | sort | uniq | wc')
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Importance of Organizing Your Projects and Scripts

As I just mentioned, `.RData` is where R will store or load the
variables when you exit or open a session. If you're working on multiple
projects, but they're all in the same directory - this means that
`.RData` could contain variables from multiple different projects. You
could inadvertently use the value stored in one project in another -
leading to spurious results.

The solution to this is to utilize different Working Directories for
your project sections.

Besides ensuring that the variables stored in `.RData` all came from
your single analysis and on some other. It' also stores your command
history for the single analysis in `.Rhistory`.

This is very different than in Jupyter Lab - where if you restarted a
Notebook - no variable values are restored from the previous time you
ran the Notebook.

**Remember**: If you're saving variables to .RData at the end of a
Session - It essentially makes these variables persistent - they'll be
restored when you re-open your session.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Exercise 1: Create a New Project Folder called `First_R` and then Create an R Markdown document in the folder.

Create a **Header**, a **Table**, and **Insert an Image**.

Copy the following block of code and then render the **Markdown
document**:

```         
data(iris)
y<-iris$Sepal.Length
x<-iris$Species

means<-tapply(y, x, mean)
s.dev<-tapply(y, x, sd)
xlevels<-1:nlevels(x)

plot(xlevels, means, ylim=c(min(y), max(y)), pch=16, axes=F, xlab="Espécie", ylab="Comprimento da Sépala", type="n", xlim=c(0.5, nlevels(x)+0.5))

axis(2, las=1)
axis(1, xlevels, levels(x))
box()

#As asas:
arrows(xlevels, means-s.dev, xlevels, means+s.dev, angle=90, code=3,
length =  0.25 , col = "grey", lwd = 7.5 )

#Os cockpits:
points(xlevels, means, pch = 19, col="grey", cex = 6 )
points(xlevels, means, pch = 19, cex = 1.5 )
points(xlevels, means, pch = 8, cex = 2.5 )
points(xlevels, means, pch = 1, cex = 4 )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

##
